title: Dark Grey Spot on Cat's Eye: Causes and Veterinary Triage
meta_desc: Noticed a dark grey spot on your cat's eye? Learn about corneal sequestrum, urgency levels, and why professional triage is essential for eye health.
content_html:
What is a Dark Grey Spot on a Cat's Eye?

Noticing a change in your cat's eye can be alarming. A dark grey or brownish-black spot on the surface of the eye is most frequently identified as a Feline Corneal Sequestrum. This is a condition where a piece of the corneal tissue dies and becomes pigmented. It is often triggered by chronic irritation, such as feline herpesvirus or eyelashes rubbing against the surface. Other possibilities include iris melanosis (flat pigment spots) or corneal scarring from a previous injury.

Urgency Level: Medium

The urgency level for a dark spot on the eye is Medium. While this is typically not a life-threatening emergency that requires an immediate midnight visit to a trauma center, eye issues can progress rapidly. If your cat is squinting, has significant discharge, or the eye appears cloudy, you should seek veterinary care within 24 to 48 hours. Left untreated, these spots can lead to deep infections or even the rupture of the eye.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Eye conditions are highly visual, and a clear, high-resolution photo is one of the most valuable tools for a triage assistant. A photo allows us to see the exact depth and texture of the spot—whether it is raised, flat, or pitted. This helps us differentiate between a surface-level issue like a sequestrum and an internal issue like a tumor, ensuring your cat receives the correct priority level for an appointment.

faqs.question: Is a corneal sequestrum painful for my cat?
faqs.answer: Yes, it often feels like having a constant foreign object or 'pebble' in the eye. You may notice your cat squinting or pawing at their face.

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops on my cat's eye spot?
faqs.answer: No. You should never use human medications or even over-the-counter pet drops without a diagnosis, as some ingredients can worsen a corneal injury.

faqs.question: Will the spot go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Usually, no. While the body may eventually 'slough' off a sequestrum, it is a long, painful process that often requires surgical intervention or specialized topical medications.
